docs.title: TRISK Desktop
docs.text:
Climate transition risk stress testing for banks - locally deployed, fully offline, audit-ready.

TRISK Desktop allows bank risk teams to run climate transition stress tests on their loan portfolios inside a controlled local environment. Sensitive portfolio data stays on bank infrastructure. Results are transparent, reproducible, and export-ready for ICAAP reports, board presentations, and regulatory submissions.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The app guides you through a numbered workflow:

  1. Upload your portfolio, asset-level data, and financial features
  2. Configure baseline and target climate scenarios, shock year, and model parameters
  3. Run the TRISK stress test engine
  4. Review portfolio results - sector breakdown, concentration risk, attribution, horizon analysis
  5. Compare outcomes across scenario families (Net Zero, Below 2C, Current Policies)
  6. Integrate TRISK outputs with your internal PD/EL estimates
  7. Export everything as Excel, CSV, or JSON

A built-in demo dataset lets you explore the full workflow immediately after installation.

docs.title: Key features
docs.text:
• Local-only deployment - all data stays on your machine, no cloud transfer
  • 114 pre-loaded scenarios from NGFS, IPR, GECO, and Mission Possible
  • Multi-horizon analysis - test shock years from 2025 to 2040
  • Risk integration - combine outputs with internal PD/EL using absolute, relative, or Z-score (Basel IRB) methods
  • Audit trail - every run gets a unique ID with full configuration metadata
  • Enterprise-hardened - non-root container, read-only filesystem, column allowlisting, formula injection protection

docs.title: Quick links
docs.text:
• Getting Started - install and run your first analysis in 5 minutes
  • Data Preparation - how to format your portfolio CSV files
  • User Guide - step-by-step walkthrough of every tab

docs.text:
• TRISK Model - the stress test engine
  • TRISK Analysis - portfolio analytics layer
  • 1in1000 Initiative - open-source climate risk methodologies

Co-funded by the European Union. Scientific Transition Risk Exercises for Stress Tests & Scenario Analysis has received funding from the European Union's Life programme under Grant No. LIFE21-GIC-DE-Stress.

docs.title: 1. Executive Summary
docs.text:
TRISK Desktop is a self-hosted web application for climate transition risk stress testing, designed for ICAAP/ILAAP preparation, board reporting, and regulatory submissions. It runs as a containerized service on an analyst's workstation or a shared internal server.

Key architecture facts for IT review:

Property Value Runtime network access None — fully air-gapped after installation Data residency 100% on-premise — no cloud, no telemetry, no callbacks Authentication HTTP Basic Auth (Caddy proxy) or institution SSO/LDAP Encryption in transit TLS 1.2+ via Caddy reverse proxy Container privileges Non-root, all capabilities dropped, custom seccomp profile PII handling Column allowlisting on upload strips unauthorized fields External dependencies at runtime None — all fonts, assets, and data vendored locally Licensing Open-source model packages (GPL-3.0 / LGPL-3.0), proprietary UI

docs.title: 2. Pre-Installation: Internal Approvals
docs.text: The following approvals should be obtained before proceeding with installation. This section maps to typical bank IT governance frameworks (ITIL, COBIT, ISO 27001).

docs.title: 2.1 Change Advisory Board (CAB) / Change Management
docs.text:
Required artifacts:

  • [ ] Change Request (CR) — standard change for new software installation
  • [ ] Risk assessment — classify as low risk (local-only, no network at runtime, no database)
  • [ ] Rollback plan — see Section 16: Decommissioning
  • [ ] Testing evidence — UAT sign-off from requesting business unit (Risk team)

Classification recommendation: Standard change (pre-approved category) if Docker is already approved in your environment. Normal change if Docker requires first-time approval.

docs.title: 2.2 Information Security / CISO Office
docs.text:
Required artifacts:

  • [ ] Data classification assessment — the application processes portfolio exposure data (likely "Confidential" or "Internal"). No PII is required by the application
  • [ ] Vendor security questionnaire — completed by 1in1000 / Theia Finance Labs
  • [ ] Container security review — review of Dockerfile, seccomp profile, and hardening controls (see Section 5)
  • [ ] Network security review — confirmation that the application makes zero outbound connections at runtime
  • [ ] Penetration test results — if required by policy for internal tools
  • [ ] DLP assessment — export controls via column allowlisting are documented

docs.title: 2.3 Architecture Review Board (ARB)
docs.text:
Required if deploying on shared infrastructure (e.g., internal VM, Kubernetes):

  • [ ] Architecture decision record (ADR) — single-container stateless application, Caddy reverse proxy, TLS termination
  • [ ] Capacity planning — 4 GB RAM, 2 CPU cores, 2 GB disk per instance
  • [ ] Integration assessment — standalone tool, no integration with core banking systems

docs.title: 2.4 Software Procurement / Vendor Management
docs.text:
• [ ] License review — open-source dependencies under GPL-3.0 and LGPL-3.0 (see Appendix A)
  • [ ] Vendor due diligence on Theia Finance Labs / 1in1000
  • [ ] Support agreement — if applicable (SLA, escalation contacts)
  • [ ] Data processing agreement (DPA) — not required for self-hosted deployment (no data leaves the institution)

docs.title: 2.5 Desktop Engineering / Endpoint Management
docs.text:
If deploying on analyst workstations:

  • [ ] Docker Desktop approval — confirm Docker is in the approved software catalog (or request addition)
  • [ ] Local admin rights — may be needed for Docker Desktop installation (one-time)
  • [ ] Disk space allocation — minimum 5 GB free
  • [ ] Port reservation — TCP 443 (production) or TCP 3838 (development)

docs.title: 3.1 Hardware (Minimum)
docs.text: Component Minimum Recommended CPU 2 cores 4 cores RAM 8 GB total (4 GB for Docker) 16 GB total Disk 5 GB free 10 GB free Display 1280 x 800 1920 x 1080

docs.title: 3.2 Operating System
docs.text: OS Supported Versions Windows 10 Pro/Enterprise (21H2+), 11 Pro/Enterprise, Server 2019+ macOS 12 Monterey or later Linux Ubuntu 22.04+, RHEL 8+, Debian 11+

docs.title: 3.3 Software Prerequisites
docs.text:
Docker deployment (recommended):

Software Version Notes Docker Desktop 24.0+ Or Docker Engine on Linux docker compose v2 Bundled with Docker Desktop Web browser Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Edge 90+ For accessing the application

Bare-metal deployment (alternative):

Software Version Notes R 4.4.1 (exact) Must match tested version Web browser Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Edge 90+ For accessing the application

docs.title: 3.4 Docker Resource Allocation
docs.text:
Configure Docker Desktop with at least:

  • Memory: 4 GB (Settings > Resources > Memory)
  • CPU: 2 cores
  • Disk image size: 20 GB

docs.title: 4.1 Installation Phase (One-Time)
docs.text:
The following outbound HTTPS (port 443) connections are required only during initial build. After the image is built, these are never needed again.

Destination Purpose Can Be Bypassed? packagemanager.posit.co R package downloads (CRAN mirror) Yes — use internal R mirror or pre-built image github.com TRISK model packages (source) Yes — vendor packages or use pre-built image storage.googleapis.com Scenario data download (CSV) Yes — transfer file manually via secure channel ghcr.io Pre-built Docker image pull (optional) Yes — build locally instead

docs.title: 4.2 Runtime Phase
docs.text:
Direction Ports Connections Inbound TCP 443 (prod) or TCP 3838 (dev) on 127.0.0.1 only Local browser only Outbound None Zero external connections

For air-gapped environments: The Docker image can be built on a staging machine with internet access, exported via docker save, transferred on approved media, and loaded via docker load on the target workstation. See Section 7.3.

docs.title: 4.3 DNS
docs.text: No DNS resolution required at runtime. The application binds to 127.0.0.1 (localhost).

docs.title: 5.1 Container Hardening
docs.text: Control Implementation Non-root execution Container runs as user trisk (nologin shell) Capability drop cap_drop: ALL — no Linux capabilities granted Privilege escalation no-new-privileges: true Filesystem Read-only root filesystem Writable paths /tmp (tmpfs, noexec, nosuid, 2 GB cap) and /data/output (noexec, nosuid) Syscall filtering Custom seccomp profile blocking ptrace, perf_event_open, keyctl, add_key, request_key, personality, userfaultfd, process_vm_readv/writev Process limits PID limit: 256 File descriptor limits 1024 soft, 4096 hard Memory limits 4 GB hard cap, 4 GB swap cap (prevents OOM-swap spiral) CPU limits 2.0 cores Log rotation 10 MB max, 3 files (prevents disk exhaustion)

docs.title: 5.2 Network Isolation
docs.text:
[Analyst Browser] ──HTTPS (443)──> [Caddy Proxy] ──HTTP (3838)──> [TRISK App]
      │                                  │                              │
   localhost                        trisk-host                    trisk-internal
   only                            network                       network (no internet)
  • The TRISK application container is on an internal: true Docker network — it cannot reach the internet
  • Only the Caddy reverse proxy is exposed to the host, on 127.0.0.1:443
  • Caddy terminates TLS and forwards to TRISK on the internal network

docs.title: 5.3 Application Security
docs.text: Control Implementation Input validation Column allowlisting on CSV upload — unauthorized columns stripped Output control Column allowlisting on export — DLP-compatible File type validation Server-side MIME type checking Formula injection Sanitization on Excel export XSS protection htmlEscape() on all user-supplied data CSP Content-Security-Policy header blocking external scripts Error handling Sanitized error messages — no stack traces exposed Session management 30-minute idle timeout Upload size 50 MB maximum Audit logging Structured JSON logging of user actions

docs.title: 5.4 Supply Chain Security
docs.text: Control Implementation Base image Pinned by SHA256 digest (not floating tag) CRAN packages Locked to Posit PPM snapshot 2025-02-01 GitHub packages Pinned to exact Git commit SHAs Scenario data SHA256 checksum verification at build time Fonts Vendored locally (no CDN at runtime)

docs.title: 5.5 Security Headers (Caddy)
docs.text:
X-Frame-Options: DENY
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
Referrer-Policy: no-referrer
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains
Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=()
Server: (removed)

docs.title: 6. Pre-Installation Checklist
docs.text: Complete all items before beginning the installation procedure.

docs.title: 6.1 Governance
docs.text:
• [ ] Change request approved (Section 2.1)
  • [ ] Information Security review complete (Section 2.2)
  • [ ] Architecture review complete if shared deployment (Section 2.3)
  • [ ] License review complete (Section 2.4)
  • [ ] Docker Desktop approved and in software catalog (Section 2.5)

docs.title: 6.2 Infrastructure
docs.text:
• [ ] Target machine meets hardware requirements (Section 3.1)
  • [ ] Operating system is on supported list (Section 3.2)
  • [ ] Docker Desktop installed and running (Section 3.3)
  • [ ] Docker memory allocation set to 4 GB minimum (Section 3.4)
  • [ ] Sufficient disk space (5 GB+)

docs.title: 6.3 Network
docs.text:
• [ ] Outbound HTTPS to build-time dependencies confirmed (Section 4.1) OR air-gap transfer plan agreed
  • [ ] Firewall rule for localhost TCP 443 or 3838 confirmed (Section 4.2)

docs.title: 6.4 Security
docs.text:
• [ ] TLS certificates obtained from institution PKI (Section 9)
  • [ ] Authentication credentials prepared (Section 10)
  • [ ] SIEM log forwarding configuration agreed (Section 13)
  • [ ] Vulnerability scan plan agreed (Section 14)

docs.title: 6.5 Data
docs.text:
• [ ] Scenario data file obtained (Section 11.1)
  • [ ] Sample portfolio CSV prepared for UAT (Section 11.2)
  • [ ] Output directory location agreed with analyst

docs.title: 7.1 Standard Installation (Internet Access Available)
docs.text:
# Step 1: Obtain application source
git clone https://github.com/Theia-Finance-Labs/trisk.r.docker.git
cd trisk.r.docker

# Step 2: Download scenario data
bash scripts/download_scenarios.sh

# Step 3: Create environment file
cp .env.example .env

# Step 4: Generate authentication password hash
docker run --rm caddy:2-alpine caddy hash-password --plaintext 'YOUR_SECURE_PASSWORD'
# Copy the output hash into .env as TRISK_PASS_HASH

# Step 5: Place TLS certificates (see Section 9)
cp /path/to/institution-cert.pem tls/cert.pem
cp /path/to/institution-key.pem  tls/key.pem

# Step 6: Build and start
docker compose up --build -d

# Step 7: Verify
docker compose ps          # All services should show "Up (healthy)"
curl -k https://localhost   # Should return HTTP 401 (auth required)

docs.title: 7.2 Bank Artifactory Deployment (Internal Registry)
docs.text:
If your institution uses JFrog Artifactory, Sonatype Nexus, or similar:

# Option A: Override base image at build time
echo "BASE_IMAGE=artifactory.bank.com/approved/r-ver:4.4.1" >> .env
docker compose up --build -d

# Option B: Pull pre-scanned image from internal registry
docker pull artifactory.bank.com/approved/trisk:1.0.0
docker tag artifactory.bank.com/approved/trisk:1.0.0 theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest
docker compose up -d

docs.title: 7.3 Air-Gapped Installation (No Internet on Target)
docs.text:
On the staging machine (with internet):

# Build the image
git clone https://github.com/Theia-Finance-Labs/trisk.r.docker.git
cd trisk.r.docker
bash scripts/download_scenarios.sh
docker compose build

# Export the images
docker save theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest | gzip > trisk-app.tar.gz
docker save caddy:2-alpine | gzip > caddy-proxy.tar.gz

# Transfer trisk-app.tar.gz, caddy-proxy.tar.gz, and the full project
# directory to the target machine via approved media (USB, SFTP, etc.)

On the target machine (air-gapped):

# Load the images
docker load < trisk-app.tar.gz
docker load < caddy-proxy.tar.gz

# Configure environment
cd trisk.r.docker
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with authentication hash and settings

# Place TLS certificates
cp /path/to/cert.pem tls/cert.pem
cp /path/to/key.pem  tls/key.pem

# Start (no --build needed, images are pre-loaded)
docker compose up -d

docs.title: 7.4 Development Mode (For UAT / Testing)
docs.text:
For testing without TLS/auth (localhost only):

docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d
# Access at http://localhost:3838 (no TLS, no auth)

Do not use development mode in production.

docs.title: 8. Installation Procedure — Bare-Metal R (Alternative)
docs.text:
Use this if Docker is not approved in your environment. See INSTALL.md in the repository root for the full step-by-step procedure, including:

  1. R 4.4.1 installation
  2. Package installation from Posit PPM snapshot
  3. GitHub package installation (trisk.model, trisk.analysis)
  4. Scenario data download
  5. Path configuration in global.R
  6. Launch command

Note: Bare-metal installations do not include the container hardening controls described in Section 5. Additional endpoint security measures should be applied per institutional policy.

docs.title: 9.1 Obtain Certificates
docs.text:
Request certificates from your institution's PKI team:

  • Certificate: PEM-encoded X.509 certificate (.pem or .crt)
  • Private key: PEM-encoded private key (.pem or .key)
  • Subject: CN=localhost or the hostname where TRISK will be accessed
  • SAN: Include localhost and 127.0.0.1 in Subject Alternative Names

docs.title: 9.2 Install Certificates
docs.text:
# Place files in the tls/ directory (gitignored)
cp institution-cert.pem tls/cert.pem
cp institution-key.pem  tls/key.pem

# Verify permissions (key must be readable by Caddy container)
chmod 644 tls/cert.pem
chmod 600 tls/key.pem

docs.title: 9.3 Self-Signed Certificates (Development Only)
docs.text:
For testing environments where institution certificates are not yet available:

openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout tls/key.pem -out tls/cert.pem \
  -sha256 -days 365 -nodes \
  -subj "/CN=localhost" \
  -addext "subjectAltName=DNS:localhost,IP:127.0.0.1"

docs.title: 10.1 HTTP Basic Auth (Default)
docs.text:
# Generate bcrypt password hash
docker run --rm caddy:2-alpine caddy hash-password --plaintext 'SecurePassword123!'

# Add to .env
TRISK_USER=analyst
TRISK_PASS_HASH=$2a$14$xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

docs.title: 10.2 SSO / LDAP Integration (Institutional)
docs.text:
For enterprise SSO, replace the Caddy basic auth block in Caddyfile with your institution's authentication proxy (e.g., Keycloak, Azure AD, Okta). The Caddy reverse proxy layer supports any header-based or forward-auth mechanism.

Contact 1in1000 for SSO integration guidance specific to your IdP.

docs.title: 10.3 Multi-User Access
docs.text: For shared server deployments, configure separate credentials per user or integrate with institutional LDAP. The application is stateless — multiple users can access simultaneously with independent sessions.

docs.title: 11.1 Scenario Data
docs.text:
The TRISK model requires a scenario dataset containing NGFS, GECO, IPR, and Mission Possible pathways.

Source: https://storage.googleapis.com/crispy-public-data/trisk_inputs/scenarios.csv SHA256: b181045bd27628e427d541ddffc860a63e3b3f8148ccaa01d708cfbbc29b4b56 Size: ~200 MB

For air-gapped environments, download on a connected machine and transfer via approved media.

Verification:

shasum -a 256 data/scenarios/scenarios.csv
# Must match: b181045bd27628e427d541ddffc860a63e3b3f8148ccaa01d708cfbbc29b4b56

docs.title: 11.2 Portfolio Data (User-Supplied)
docs.text:
Analysts upload their portfolio via the application UI. The CSV must contain:

Column Type Description company_id String Unique company identifier sector String TRISK sector classification technology String TRISK technology classification country_iso2 String ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code exposure_value_usd Numeric Loan exposure in USD term Numeric Loan maturity (years) loss_given_default Numeric LGD estimate (0-1)

A demo dataset is available in the application ("Load Demo Data" button) for UAT.

docs.title: 11.3 Directory Structure
docs.text:
data/
├── scenarios/
│   └── scenarios.csv        # Pre-downloaded (Section 11.1)
├── input/                   # Analyst CSV uploads (read-only mount)
└── output/                  # Results export directory (writable)

docs.title: 12.1 Container Health Check
docs.text:
# All services should show "Up (healthy)"
docker compose ps

# Expected output:
# trisk-proxy   caddy:2-alpine   Up      127.0.0.1:443->443/tcp
# trisk-app     trisk:latest     Up (healthy)    3838/tcp

docs.title: 12.2 Application Access
docs.text:
1. Open https://localhost in the browser (or http://localhost:3838 in dev mode)
  2. Enter credentials when prompted
  3. The TRISK Desktop dashboard should load with the sidebar navigation

docs.title: 12.3 Functional Verification
docs.text:
1. Click "Load Demo Data" on the Upload Data tab
  2. Navigate to Configure Scenario — verify scenario options appear
  3. Click Run Analysis — verify the model executes (30-60 seconds)
  4. Review Portfolio Results — verify charts and KPI cards render
  5. Test Export — download results as Excel/CSV and verify file contents

docs.title: 12.4 Security Verification
docs.text:
# Verify no outbound connections
docker exec trisk-app wget -q --spider https://google.com 2>&1
# Should fail (no internet access from internal network)

# Verify non-root execution
docker exec trisk-app whoami
# Should return: trisk

# Verify read-only filesystem
docker exec trisk-app touch /test 2>&1
# Should fail: Read-only file system

# Verify seccomp profile
docker exec trisk-app cat /proc/self/status | grep Seccomp
# Should return: Seccomp:  2 (filter mode)

docs.title: 12.5 Sign-Off
docs.text: Test Pass/Fail Tester Date Container health check Application access Demo data load Model execution Results rendering Export functionality No outbound network Non-root execution Read-only filesystem Seccomp active

docs.title: 13.1 Docker Log Driver
docs.text:
By default, logs use the json-file driver with rotation. For SIEM integration, switch to syslog:

# In docker-compose.yml, replace the logging block:
logging:
  driver: syslog
  options:
    syslog-address: "tcp://siem.bank.com:514"
    tag: "trisk-app"
    syslog-facility: "local0"

docs.title: 13.2 Audit Events
docs.text:
The application logs the following events in structured JSON format:

  • Portfolio upload (file name, row count, columns detected)
  • Analysis execution (scenario, parameters, duration)
  • Results export (format, row count)
  • Session start/end
  • Errors (sanitized, no stack traces)

docs.title: 13.3 Caddy Access Logs
docs.text:
Caddy logs all HTTP requests including:

  • Client IP (always 127.0.0.1 for localhost deployments)
  • Request path and method
  • Response status code
  • Authentication username
  • Timestamp

docs.title: 14.1 Image Scanning
docs.text:
Scan the built image before deployment:

# Using Trivy (recommended)
trivy image theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest

# Using Grype
grype theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest

Policy: Remediate all HIGH and CRITICAL findings before production deployment. MEDIUM findings should be documented with risk acceptance.

docs.title: 14.2 Ongoing Scanning
docs.text:
Schedule periodic scans (monthly recommended) to detect newly disclosed CVEs in base image packages:

# Rebuild with latest base image patches
docker compose build --no-cache
trivy image theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest

docs.title: 14.3 SBOM Generation
docs.text:
Generate a Software Bill of Materials for compliance:

# Using Syft
syft theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest -o spdx-json > trisk-sbom.spdx.json

# Using Trivy
trivy image --format spdx-json theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest > trisk-sbom.spdx.json

docs.title: 15.1 What to Back Up
docs.text: Component Location Frequency Configuration .env, Caddyfile, tls/ After any change Analysis results data/output/ After each analysis run Portfolio data data/input/ Per analyst policy

docs.title: 15.2 What Does NOT Need Backup
docs.text:
• Docker images (rebuildable from source)
  • Scenario data (re-downloadable, checksummed)
  • Application state (stateless — no database)

docs.title: 15.3 Recovery Procedure
docs.text:
1. Restore configuration files (.env, Caddyfile, tls/)
  2. Rebuild Docker images: docker compose up --build -d
  3. Restore data/output/ from backup if needed

docs.title: 16. Decommissioning
docs.text:
To completely remove TRISK Desktop from a workstation:

# Stop and remove containers
docker compose down -v

# Remove Docker images
docker rmi theia-finance-labs/trisk:latest
docker rmi caddy:2-alpine

# Remove application directory
rm -rf trisk.r.docker/

# Remove Docker volumes (if any remain)
docker volume prune

Data retention: Ensure analysis results in data/output/ are archived per institutional data retention policy before deletion.

docs.title: 17.1 Container Fails to Start
docs.text:
# Check container logs
docker compose logs trisk

# Common issues:
# - "port 443 already in use" → another service on port 443, change port in docker-compose.yml
# - "TRISK_PASS_HASH not set" → generate and add to .env (Section 10.1)
# - OOM killed → increase Docker memory allocation to 4 GB+

docs.title: 17.2 Application Loads but Shows Errors
docs.text:
# Check Shiny application logs
docker logs trisk-app 2>&1 | tail -50

# Enable debug logging
echo "TRISK_DEBUG_LOG=true" >> .env
docker compose up -d

docs.title: 17.3 TLS Certificate Errors
docs.text:
• Verify certificate files exist in tls/ directory
  • Verify certificate has localhost or 127.0.0.1 in SAN
  • For self-signed certs, browser will show warning — add exception or use institution CA

docs.title: 17.4 Performance Issues
docs.text:
• Ensure Docker has 4 GB RAM allocated
  • Large portfolios (>10,000 loans) may require 8 GB RAM
  • Check docker stats for resource utilization

docs.title: Template: Change Request Summary
docs.text:
Title: Install TRISK Desktop — Climate Transition Risk Stress Testing Tool
Requester: [Risk team / Credit Risk / ICAAP team]
Category: Standard Change — New Software Installation
Risk Level: Low
Impact: Single workstation / No integration with core systems
Description: Self-hosted, containerized application for climate transition
  risk stress testing. Runs locally with zero outbound network access.
  Used for ICAAP preparation, regulatory submissions, and board reporting.
Rollback: docker compose down -v && rm -rf trisk.r.docker/
Testing: UAT with demo data, functional verification checklist (Section 12)

docs.title: Template: InfoSec Review Summary
docs.text:
Application: TRISK Desktop v1.0
Vendor: 1in1000 (Theia Finance Labs)
Data classification: Confidential (portfolio exposure data)
Network: Air-gapped at runtime — zero outbound connections
Authentication: HTTP Basic Auth (Caddy) / SSO-compatible
Encryption: TLS 1.2+ via Caddy reverse proxy
Container: Non-root, all caps dropped, seccomp filtered, read-only fs
PII: Not required. Column allowlisting strips unauthorized fields on upload.
Audit: Structured JSON logging, SIEM-compatible

docs.title: Appendix D: Environment Variable Reference
docs.text:
Variable Required Default Description TRISK_USER Yes (prod) analyst HTTP Basic Auth username TRISK_PASS_HASH Yes (prod) — Bcrypt hash for HTTP Basic Auth BASE_IMAGE No rocker/r-ver:4.4.1@sha256:... Override for bank artifactory SHINY_LOG_LEVEL No WARN Log verbosity: WARN or TRACE TRISK_DEBUG_LOG No false Enable debug logging: true or false

Document prepared by 1in1000 Consulting for institutional deployment of TRISK Desktop. For support: contact your 1in1000 engagement lead.

docs.title: Data Preparation
docs.text: TRISK Desktop accepts four CSV input files. Only the portfolio file is strictly required - the app includes bundled asset-level and scenario data that covers most use cases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Dataset Required? Rows (typical) Purpose Portfolio Yes 10 - 10,000 Your loan book with company exposures Assets Recommended 100,000+ Company production data (bundled dataset included) Financial Features Optional 1,000+ Company PD, margins, volatility for Merton model Scenarios Optional 100,000+ Climate transition pathways (114 scenarios pre-loaded)

docs.title: Portfolio CSV
docs.text:
Your loan book with exposures to climate-sensitive sectors.

Column Type Required Description Example company_id integer Yes Unique company identifier 374444 company_name string Yes Company name (used for fuzzy matching) ACME Energy Ltd. sector string No Sector classification (enriched from assets if missing) Power technology string No Technology (enriched from assets if missing) CoalCap country_iso2 string Yes ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code DE exposure_value_usd numeric Yes Loan exposure in USD 5000000 term integer Yes Loan term in years (1-5) 3 loss_given_default numeric Yes LGD ratio (0 to 1) 0.45

Sector and technology columns

If your portfolio only has company_id and exposure data, the app will automatically enrich it with sector and technology information from the assets database. You do not need to manually classify companies.

docs.title: Supported sectors and technologies
docs.text: Sector Technologies Power CoalCap, OilCap, GasCap, NuclearCap, HydroCap, RenewablesCap Oil&Gas Oil, Gas Coal Coal Automotive Electric, FuelCell, Hybrid, ICE Steel Integrated facility, Electric arc furnace

docs.title: Assets CSV
docs.text:
Company-level production data linking portfolio companies to physical assets. A comprehensive global dataset is bundled with the app covering thousands of companies. Upload your own only if you have proprietary asset data.

Column Type Required Description company_id integer Yes Must match portfolio company_name string Yes Company name asset_id string Yes Unique asset identifier country_iso2 string Yes Asset location (ISO alpha-2) sector string Yes Production sector technology string Yes Technology type production_year integer Yes Year of production data capacity numeric Yes Asset capacity capacity_factor numeric Yes Utilization factor (0-1) emission_factor numeric Yes Emissions per unit produced production_unit string Yes Unit of production (MW, GJ, tonnes)

docs.title: Financial Features CSV
docs.text:
Company financial metrics used in the Merton structural model for PD estimation.

Column Type Required Description company_id integer Yes Must match portfolio and assets pd numeric Yes Current probability of default net_profit_margin numeric Yes Net profit margin ratio debt_equity_ratio numeric Yes Debt to equity ratio volatility numeric Yes Asset value volatility

docs.title: Scenarios CSV
docs.text:
Climate transition scenarios are pre-loaded with 114 scenarios from NGFS, IPR, GECO, and Mission Possible. Upload custom scenarios only if you have proprietary pathway data.

See the Scenario Library reference for the full list of bundled scenarios.

docs.title: Data quality tips
docs.text:
Column names are case-sensitive

The column company_id is not the same as Company_ID. Use exact lowercase names as shown above.

  • company_id must match across portfolio, assets, and financial features files
  • No missing values in required columns - the app will flag rows with NAs
  • Country codes must be ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 (two uppercase letters: DE, US, FR, MN)
  • Numeric columns must contain numbers only - no currency symbols, commas, or text
  • File format: UTF-8 encoded CSV with comma delimiters
  • Upload limit: 100 MB per file, 500,000 rows maximum

Column allowlisting

For security, the app automatically strips any columns not in the expected list. Extra columns (including potentially sensitive ones) are removed on upload and never stored.

docs.title: Download templates
docs.text: The app provides downloadable CSV templates with the correct column headers. On the Upload Data tab, click Download Template CSV under each data panel to get a blank template.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Get TRISK Desktop running and produce your first stress test results in under 10 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Requirement Details Docker Desktop Download for Windows/Mac or Docker Engine for Linux RAM 4 GB minimum allocated to Docker (8 GB recommended) Disk ~3 GB for the Docker image Browser Chrome, Edge, Firefox, or Safari

No R or programming knowledge required

Everything runs inside the Docker container. You only need Docker and a web browser.

docs.title: Install and launch
docs.text:
# 1. Get the code
git clone https://github.com/Theia-Finance-Labs/trisk.r.docker.git
cd trisk.r.docker

# 2. Build and start (first time takes ~10 minutes)
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d

# 3. Check it's running
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml logs -f

Wait until you see:

Listening on http://0.0.0.0:3838

Then open your browser to http://localhost:3838

docs.title: Run the demo
docs.text:
Once the app loads:

  1. Click 1. Upload Data in the sidebar
  2. Click the green Load Demo Data from trisk.model button
  3. Click 2. Configure Analysis - default scenarios are already selected
  4. Click 3. Run Analysis, then click the Run TRISK Analysis button
  5. Wait ~30 seconds for the model to complete
  6. Explore your results in 4. Portfolio Results

The demo loads a synthetic 4-company portfolio across Oil&Gas, Coal, and Power sectors with matching asset-level production data.

docs.title: Using your own data
docs.text:
To analyze your own portfolio:

  1. Prepare your CSV files following the Data Preparation guide
  2. Either upload through the browser interface, or place files in data/input/ before starting the container
  3. Follow the same workflow: Upload, Configure, Run, Review

docs.title: Stopping and restarting
docs.text:
# Stop
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml down

# Restart (no rebuild needed)
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d

# Rebuild after updating the code
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Data Preparation - format your portfolio files
  • Step 1: Upload Data - detailed upload walkthrough
  • Security Architecture - what your IT team needs to know
  • Troubleshooting - if something doesn't work

docs.title: Security Architecture
docs.text: TRISK Desktop is designed for deployment on bank workstations and controlled internal environments.

docs.title: Data containment
docs.text:
• All processing happens locally inside the Docker container
  • No data leaves your machine at runtime - no telemetry, no cloud calls, no analytics
  • Input data is mounted read-only
  • Network access is not required after the initial image build
  • The application binds to localhost only (127.0.0.1)

docs.title: Container hardening
docs.text: Control Implementation Non-root execution Application runs as unprivileged trisk user with nologin shell Read-only filesystem Root filesystem is read-only; only /tmp and /data/output are writable Capability drop All Linux capabilities dropped (cap_drop: ALL) Privilege escalation Blocked (no-new-privileges: true) Resource limits Memory 4 GB, CPU 2 cores, PID limit 256 Log rotation 10 MB x 3 files maximum Output restrictions Output volume mounted with noexec,nosuid flags Network isolation internal: true Docker network - no outbound internet

docs.title: Application security
docs.text:
Upload controls

Uploaded files are validated against strict column allowlists. Extra columns - including potentially sensitive ones like employee names, account numbers, or internal IDs - are automatically stripped and never stored.

Export controls

Exported results pass through a separate column allowlist. Only expected output columns are included. Character columns are sanitized against formula injection (CWE-1236) - cells starting with =, +, @, or tab characters are prefixed with a single quote.

Error handling

User-facing errors are sanitized (shiny.sanitize.errors = TRUE). No stack traces, file paths, or internal state is exposed to the browser. Detailed errors are logged server-side only.

Audit logging

All uploads, analysis runs, and exports are logged to /data/output/audit/trisk-audit.jsonl in structured JSON-lines format with ISO-8601 timestamps and session identifiers.

docs.title: Supply chain security
docs.text:
• Base Docker image pinned by SHA-256 digest (not just tag)
  • CRAN packages locked to a Posit PPM date snapshot (immutable)
  • GitHub packages pinned to exact commit SHAs
  • Scenario data verified against SHA-256 checksum at build time
  • All fonts vendored locally - no runtime CDN requests

docs.title: Authentication (production deployment)
docs.text:
The production docker-compose.yml includes a Caddy reverse proxy with:

  • TLS encryption using bank-provided certificates
  • HTTP Basic Authentication with bcrypt-hashed passwords
  • Security headers: HSTS, X-Frame-Options DENY, CSP, no-referrer, permissions policy
  • For SSO/LDAP integration, replace the Basic Auth block in the Caddyfile with your proxy-level authentication

docs.title: Deployment checklist
docs.text:
1. Replace TLS certificates in tls/ with bank-issued certs
  2. Generate auth password hash: docker run --rm caddy:2-alpine caddy hash-password
  3. Set TRISK_PASS_HASH in .env
  4. Override BASE_IMAGE if using a bank artifactory
  5. Run trivy image scan on the built image
  6. Configure Docker log driver for SIEM forwarding if required
  7. Review seccomp profile against bank security policy

docs.title: Known limitations
docs.text:
CSP allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval

The Content Security Policy permits unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval in script and style directives. This is a known requirement of the Shiny framework, which dynamically generates and injects JavaScript. Input validation and output sanitization provide defense-in-depth.

External links to documentation sites (theia-finance-labs.github.io, 1in1000.com) appear in the About and Documentation tabs. In air-gapped deployments, these links will not resolve but do not affect functionality.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions when running TRISK Desktop.

docs.title: Docker Desktop is not running
docs.text:
Symptom: Cannot connect to the Docker daemon

Fix: Open Docker Desktop and wait for it to fully start. The whale icon in your system tray should be steady (not animating).

docs.title: Build fails with network error
docs.text:
Symptom: Could not resolve host during docker compose build

Fix: Internet access is required during the build step to download R packages. After build completes, the app runs fully offline.

docs.title: Build fails with disk space error
docs.text:
Symptom: no space left on device

Fix: The TRISK image requires ~3 GB. Free disk space or run docker system prune to remove unused Docker images and containers.

docs.title: App doesn't load in browser
docs.text:
Check the container is running:

docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml ps

Check the logs:

docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml logs trisk

Look for Listening on http://0.0.0.0:3838 to confirm the app started.

docs.title: Port 3838 already in use
docs.text:
Symptom: Container starts but browser shows connection refused

Mac/LinuxWindows 
lsof -i :3838

netstat -ano | findstr :3838

Fix: Stop the process using port 3838, or change the port in docker-compose.dev.yml:

ports:
  - "127.0.0.1:4000:3838"  # Use port 4000 instead

Then access the app at http://localhost:4000.

docs.title: Out of memory
docs.text:
Symptom: Container killed unexpectedly, or R session crashes during analysis

Fix:

  1. Open Docker Desktop > Settings > Resources > increase Memory to 8 GB+
  2. For very large portfolios (1000+ companies), consider splitting into sector-level batches

docs.title: Upload validation errors
docs.text:
Column names are case-sensitive

company_id is not the same as Company_ID or CompanyId. Use exact lowercase names.

Missing required columns - check column names match the Data Dictionary exactly.

Wrong data types - ensure numeric columns contain only numbers (no currency symbols, commas, or text).

Empty file - verify the CSV is not empty and uses comma delimiters (not semicolons or tabs).

docs.title: No companies matched
docs.text:
Symptom: Analysis runs but produces 0 results

Cause: The company_id values in your portfolio don't match any entries in the assets database.

Fix:

  • Verify your portfolio uses the same company_id values as the assets file
  • If using the bundled assets database, check that your companies are covered
  • Try the demo data first to confirm the app works, then troubleshoot your data

docs.title: Scenario not available
docs.text:
Symptom: Warning message about missing scenario for selected geography

Fix: Some scenarios are only available for "Global" geography. Switch to Global in the Configure tab, or check the Scenario Library for available geographies.

docs.title: Analysis takes too long
docs.text:
Expected run times:

Portfolio size Approximate time 10 companies ~10 seconds 100 companies ~30-60 seconds 500 companies ~2-5 minutes 1000+ companies 5-15 minutes

Multiple shock years multiply the time proportionally.

To speed up analysis:

  • Reduce the number of shock years (use 1 instead of 3)
  • Increase Docker memory and CPU allocation
  • Split large portfolios into sector-specific batches

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text:
Can I run TRISK Desktop offline?

Yes. After the initial Docker build (which requires internet), the app runs fully offline with no external network calls.

What browsers are supported?

Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Edge 90+, Safari 14+.

Can I use my own scenario data?

Yes. Upload a CSV with the expected columns on the Upload Data tab. See the Data Dictionary for the schema.

How do I update to a new version?

Pull the latest code and rebuild:

git pull
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d
Where are results stored?

Results are stored in the data/output/ directory on your host machine (mounted into the container). Audit logs go to data/output/audit/.

Can multiple users share one installation?

The development configuration supports one user at a time. The production configuration with Caddy proxy supports multiple authenticated users, but Shiny sessions are single-threaded per connection.

docs.title: PD Analysis Overhaul — Design Document
docs.text: Date: 2026-03-14 Status: Approved

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Redesign the Exposure PD Analysis tab to use relative percentage PD change, add descriptive legends, remove scatter from the density plot, and introduce a new PD Change vs Exposure scatter plot.

docs.title: 1. PD Change Formula Change
docs.text: From: (pd_shock - pd_baseline) * 100 (absolute, percentage points) To: (pd_shock - pd_baseline) / pd_baseline * 100 (relative, percentage change)

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
• vb_pd_change value box — display "XX.X%" instead of "X.XX pp"
  • Summary card row — merge "PD Change (pp)" into existing "PD Change (%)", drop the pp card
  • plot_pd_sector_enhanced — x-axis label and data in relative %
  • plot_pd_distribution — x-axis label and data in relative %
  • New plot_pd_vs_exposure — built with relative % from the start

docs.title: Edge Cases
docs.text:
• pd_baseline = 0 → return 0 (no meaningful relative change)
  • Sign preserved: pd_shock > pd_baseline → positive (deterioration); pd_shock < pd_baseline → negative (improvement)

docs.title: Bar chart (plot_pd_sector_enhanced)
docs.text: Expanded subtitle: - Gray whiskers = 95% confidence interval (±1.96 SE across companies × shock years) - Black tick marks (━) = average PD change per individual shock year

docs.title: Density plot (plot_pd_distribution)
docs.text: Label the vertical lines directly: - Dashed black line: overall portfolio average, labeled with value - Dotted black lines: per-shock-year averages, labeled with year identifier

docs.title: 3. Clean Density Plot
docs.text: Remove all geom_point bubble overlay, bubble sizing, jitter, and density_y interpolation code. Retain: - geom_density() with light green fill and TRISK_HEX_GREEN line - geom_vline() for overall average (dashed, labeled) - geom_vline() for per-year averages (dotted, labeled) - Remove sector color legend (no scatter points to color)

docs.title: 4. New Scatter — PD Change vs Exposure
docs.text: New plot_pd_vs_exposure in a full-width row below the two side-by-side plots: - X-axis: PD Change (%) — relative percentage change - Y-axis: Exposure (USD) — formatted with M/K suffixes - Points: one per company×year, colored by sector (SECTOR_COLORS) - Tooltips: company name, sector, PD change %, exposure, shock year - Top-right corner = highest risk concentration

docs.title: Files to Modify
docs.text: File Changes app/server.R PD formula in value box, enhanced bar chart, density plot; new scatter plot app/ui.R Add plotlyOutput("plot_pd_vs_exposure") in full-width row; update summary card app/global.R Optional: add helper function for relative PD change

docs.title: PD Analysis Overhaul — Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For Claude: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task.

Goal: Change PD change from absolute (pp) to relative (%), add plot legends, clean up density plot, and add PD Change vs Exposure scatter plot.

Architecture: Four independent changes to the Exposure PD Analysis tab in a Shiny/R app. All PD change calculations shift from (shock - baseline) * 100 to (shock - baseline) / baseline * 100 with a zero-baseline guard. A new scatter plot replaces the bubble overlay removed from the density plot.

Tech Stack: R, Shiny, ggplot2, plotly (via ggplotly), shinydashboard

docs.title: Task 1: Change vb_pd_change Value Box to Relative %
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/server.R:1992-2011 (the vb_pd_change renderValueBox block)

Step 1: Update the value box formula and display

Replace the absolute formula with relative percentage change. Change the label and display format.

In app/server.R, replace lines 1992-2011 with:

  output$vb_pd_change <- renderValueBox({
    if (is.null(rv$results)) {
      return(valueBox("---", "PD Change, Exposure-Weighted (%)", icon = icon("balance-scale"), color = "blue"))
    }
    val <- NA_real_
    df <- rv$results
    if (all(c("pd_baseline", "pd_shock") %in% names(df))) {
      exp_col <- if ("exposure_value_usd" %in% names(df)) df$exposure_value_usd else rep(1, nrow(df))
      total_exp <- sum(exp_col, na.rm = TRUE)
      if (total_exp > 0) {
        weighted_baseline <- sum(df$pd_baseline * exp_col, na.rm = TRUE) / total_exp
        weighted_change   <- sum((df$pd_shock - df$pd_baseline) * exp_col, na.rm = TRUE) / total_exp
        val <- if (weighted_baseline != 0) weighted_change / weighted_baseline * 100 else 0
      }
    }
    display_val <- if (!is.na(val)) paste0(smart_round(val), "%") else "N/A"
    valueBox(tags$span(display_val,
                       title = paste0("Full precision: ", smart_round(val), "%"),
                       style = "cursor: help;"),
             "PD Change, Exposure-Weighted (%)",
             icon = if (!is.na(val) && val > 0) icon("arrow-up") else icon("arrow-down"),
             color = if (!is.na(val) && val > 0.01) "red" else if (!is.na(val) && val < -0.01) "green" else "blue")
  })

Step 2: Rebuild Docker and verify value box renders

Run: docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d Expected: Container starts, no R errors in docker logs trisk-app-dev

Step 3: Commit

git add app/server.R
git commit -m "refactor: change vb_pd_change value box from absolute pp to relative %"

docs.title: Task 2: Update Summary Cards — Drop pp Card, Keep Only % Card
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/server.R:2462-2513 (the summary cards fluidRow)

Step 1: Remove the "PD Change (pp)" card and keep only "PD Change (%)"

In app/server.R, in the fluidRow(...) block starting at line 2496, delete the make_card("PD Change (pp)", ...) call (lines 2504-2506). The existing "PD Change (%)" card at lines 2507-2510 already uses the relative formula and stays as-is.

The resulting fluidRow should have 5 cards: Companies Analyzed, Total Exposure, Max PD Shock, PD Change (%), Total EL Change.

Also delete the now-unused pd_change_pp calculation block (lines 2462-2471) and the pd_pp_class variable since nothing references them anymore.

Step 2: Rebuild and verify

Run: docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d Load demo data → run analysis → check that Summary cards show 5 items with "PD Change (%)" displaying a percentage value.

Step 3: Commit

git add app/server.R
git commit -m "refactor: remove absolute PD Change (pp) summary card, keep only relative %"

docs.title: Task 3: Update Bar Chart to Use Relative % with Expanded Legend
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/server.R:2602-2665 (the plot_pd_sector_enhanced renderPlotly block)

Step 1: Change the PD change formula from absolute to relative

Replace line 2609: 

    df <- df %>% mutate(pd_change_pp = (pd_shock - pd_baseline) * 100)
With: 
    df <- df %>% mutate(
      pd_change_pct = ifelse(pd_baseline != 0,
                             (pd_shock - pd_baseline) / pd_baseline * 100,
                             0)
    )

Then replace ALL references to pd_change_pp with pd_change_pct in this block (sector_stats, year_sector_means, and the ggplot).

Step 2: Update the axis label and expand the subtitle legend

Replace the labs(...) call with: 

      labs(
        title = "PD Change by Sector",
        subtitle = paste0(
          "Bars = mean across shock years | ",
          "Whiskers = 95% CI (\u00b11.96 SE) | ",
          "Ticks (\u2501) = per-year avg"
        ),
        x = "", y = "Avg PD Change (%)"
      )

Step 3: Rebuild and verify the bar chart

Run: docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d Load demo → run → go to Exposure PD Analysis tab → verify bar chart shows % values, expanded subtitle is readable.

Step 4: Commit

git add app/server.R
git commit -m "refactor: bar chart uses relative PD change %, add expanded legend subtitle"

docs.title: Task 4: Clean Density Plot — Remove Scatter, Label Avg Lines
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/server.R:2667-2765 (the plot_pd_distribution renderPlotly block)

Step 1: Rewrite the density plot without scatter overlay

Replace the entire output$plot_pd_distribution <- renderPlotly({...}) block with:

  # Plot 2: PD Distribution — clean density curve with labeled averages
  output$plot_pd_distribution <- renderPlotly({
    req(rv$results_by_year)
    df <- all_years_data()
    req(nrow(df) > 0, "sector" %in% names(df), "pd_baseline" %in% names(df), "pd_shock" %in% names(df))

    # Compute relative PD change (%)
    df <- df %>% mutate(
      pd_change_pct = ifelse(pd_baseline != 0,
                             (pd_shock - pd_baseline) / pd_baseline * 100,
                             0)
    )

    # Overall average
    overall_mean <- mean(df$pd_change_pct, na.rm = TRUE)

    # Per-year averages
    year_means <- df %>%
      group_by(shock_year) %>%
      summarise(year_mean = mean(pd_change_pct, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

    # Get density peak for label positioning
    dens <- density(df$pd_change_pct, na.rm = TRUE, n = 512)
    y_top <- max(dens$y) * 0.95

    # Build clean density plot
    p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = pd_change_pct)) +
      # Density curve — light green
      geom_density(fill = "#C5E1A5", color = TRISK_HEX_GREEN, alpha = 0.3, linewidth = 0.5) +
      # Overall average — dashed black
      geom_vline(xintercept = overall_mean, linetype = "dashed",
                 color = "black", linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.5) +
      # Per-year averages — dotted black
      geom_vline(data = year_means, aes(xintercept = year_mean),
                 linetype = "dotted", color = "black", linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.4) +
      labs(
        title = "Distribution of PD Changes",
        subtitle = paste0(
          "Dashed = overall avg (", round(overall_mean, 1), "%) | ",
          "Dotted = per-year avg: ",
          paste0(year_means$shock_year, " (", round(year_means$year_mean, 1), "%)",
                 collapse = ", ")
        ),
        x = "PD Change (%)", y = "Density"
      ) +
      trisk_plot_theme() +
      theme(
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 8, color = "gray50"),
        legend.position = "none"
      )

    tc <- plotly_theme_colors(input)
    ggplotly(p, tooltip = c("x", "y")) %>%
      layout(
        font = list(family = "Inter, sans-serif", size = 12, color = tc$font_color),
        paper_bgcolor = tc$paper_bgcolor,
        plot_bgcolor = tc$plot_bgcolor,
        hoverlabel = PLOTLY_HOVERLABEL
      ) %>%
      config(displayModeBar = PLOTLY_CONFIG$displayModeBar,
             modeBarButtonsToRemove = PLOTLY_CONFIG$modeBarButtonsToRemove,
             displaylogo = PLOTLY_CONFIG$displaylogo)
  })

Step 2: Rebuild and verify the density plot

Run: docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build -d Load demo → run → Exposure PD Analysis tab → verify: - No scatter bubbles on the density plot - Light green density curve present - Dashed overall average line visible - Dotted per-year lines visible - Subtitle lists each year with its average value

Step 3: Commit

git add app/server.R
git commit -m "refactor: clean density plot - remove scatter, label avg lines, use relative %"

docs.title: Task 5: Add New PD Change vs Exposure Scatter Plot
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/ui.R:461-465 (add new plotlyOutput row) - Modify: app/server.R (add new renderPlotly block after the density plot, ~line 2765)

Step 1: Add the UI output for the new scatter plot

In app/ui.R, after the existing fluidRow with the two side-by-side plots (after line 464), add a new full-width row:

Replace lines 461-465: 

              fluidRow(
                box(width = 6, plotlyOutput("plot_pd_sector_enhanced", height = "420px")),
                box(width = 6, plotlyOutput("plot_pd_distribution", height = "420px"))
              ),
              DTOutput("pd_table")
With: 
              fluidRow(
                box(width = 6, plotlyOutput("plot_pd_sector_enhanced", height = "420px")),
                box(width = 6, plotlyOutput("plot_pd_distribution", height = "420px"))
              ),
              fluidRow(
                box(width = 12, plotlyOutput("plot_pd_vs_exposure", height = "380px"))
              ),
              DTOutput("pd_table")

Step 2: Add the server-side renderPlotly for the scatter plot

In app/server.R, after the closing }) of plot_pd_distribution (after the current line 2765), add:

  # Plot 3: PD Change (%) vs Exposure — risk concentration scatter
  output$plot_pd_vs_exposure <- renderPlotly({
    req(rv$results_by_year)
    df <- all_years_data()
    req(nrow(df) > 0, "sector" %in% names(df),
        "pd_baseline" %in% names(df), "pd_shock" %in% names(df),
        "exposure_value_usd" %in% names(df))

    # Compute relative PD change (%)
    df <- df %>% mutate(
      pd_change_pct = ifelse(pd_baseline != 0,
                             (pd_shock - pd_baseline) / pd_baseline * 100,
                             0)
    )

    # Ensure company_name column exists for tooltip
    if (!"company_name" %in% names(df)) df$company_name <- NA_character_

    # Sector colours
    sectors_present <- sort(unique(df$sector))
    sector_colors <- SECTOR_COLORS[sectors_present]
    missing <- setdiff(sectors_present, names(SECTOR_COLORS))
    if (length(missing) > 0) sector_colors[missing] <- "gray50"

    p <- ggplot(df, aes(
      x = pd_change_pct,
      y = exposure_value_usd,
      color = sector,
      text = paste0(
        ifelse(!is.na(company_name), company_name, paste0("Company ", company_id)),
        "\nSector: ", sector,
        "\nPD Change: ", round(pd_change_pct, 1), "%",
        "\nExposure: $", format(round(exposure_value_usd), big.mark = ","),
        "\nShock Year: ", shock_year
      )
    )) +
      geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 3.5, shape = 16) +
      scale_color_manual(values = sector_colors, name = "Sector") +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) {
        ifelse(abs(x) >= 1e6, paste0(round(x / 1e6, 1), "M"),
        ifelse(abs(x) >= 1e3, paste0(round(x / 1e3, 0), "K"),
               as.character(round(x))))
      }) +
      labs(
        title = "PD Change vs Exposure",
        subtitle = "Top-right = high exposure + large PD increase (highest risk)",
        x = "PD Change (%)", y = "Exposure (USD)"
      ) +
      trisk_plot_theme() +
      theme(
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray50"),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 9)
      )

    tc <- plotly_theme_colors(input)
    ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text") %>%
      layout(
        font = list(family = "Inter, sans-serif", size = 12, color = tc$font_color),
        paper_bgcolor = tc$paper_bgcolor,
        plot_bgcolor = tc$plot_bgcolor,
        hoverlabel = PLOTLY_HOVERLABEL,
        legend = list(orientation = "h", y = -0.15, x = 0.5, xanchor = "center")
      ) %>%
      config(displayModeBar = PLOTLY_CONFIG$displayModeBar,
             modeBarButtonsToRemove = PLOTLY_CONFIG$modeBarButtonsToRemove,
             displaylogo = PLOTLY_CONFIG$displaylogo)
  })

docs.title: Scenario Sensitivity Analysis — Implementation Plan
docs.text:
For Claude: REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task.

Goal: Add a new "Scenario Sensitivity" tab that automatically detects alternative scenarios from the same family/vintage as the user's chosen target, runs the model for each, and displays results as distributions with confidence intervals.

Architecture: One new sidebar tab, one new module file, helper functions in global.R, and new reactive values in server.R. The tab auto-detects sibling target scenarios (same NGFS family+vintage, all pathways x all IAMs), runs each against the same baseline, and renders violin/dot plots showing the distribution of key metrics. The user's primary scenario is highlighted in the distribution.

Key Design Decisions: - Scope: All target scenarios in the same family+vintage as the user's core target. For NGFS 2024, that's 18 targets (6 pathways x 3 IAMs — NZ2050, B2DS, LD, DT, FW, NDC x GCAM, MESSAGE, REMIND). - Baseline: Same baseline for all sensitivity runs (matching app's existing convention — baseline_for_scenario() determines the correct one). - Portfolio filtering: Same sector-filtered approach as the main run — core pair sectors only (Oil&Gas, Coal, Power). Non-core pairs (Automotive, Steel) are excluded since they typically have only one target scenario variant. - Results storage: Single flat dataframe with a sensitivity_scenario column, separate from rv$results. - UI: Nearly zero-effort — one-click run after main analysis, auto-detected scenario list shown before run.

docs.title: Task 1: Add Scenario Parsing Helpers to global.R
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/global.R (after scenario_family(), ~line 748)

Step 1: Add parse_scenario_code() function

This function decomposes a scenario code into its constituent parts (family, vintage, IAM model, pathway):

#' Parse a scenario code into structured components
#' @return list(family, vintage, iam, pathway, family_prefix) or NULL if unparseable
parse_scenario_code <- function(code) {
  # NGFS pattern: NGFS{year}[_]{model}[_]{pathway}
  m <- regmatches(code, regexec("^NGFS(\\d{4})[_]?(GCAM|MESSAGE|REMIND)[_]?(.+)$", code))[[1]]
  if (length(m) == 4) {
    return(list(family = "NGFS", vintage = m[2], iam = m[3], pathway = m[4],
                family_prefix = paste0("NGFS", m[2])))
  }
  # GECO pattern: GECO{year}_{pathway}
  m <- regmatches(code, regexec("^GECO(\\d{4})[_](.+)$", code))[[1]]
  if (length(m) == 3) {
    return(list(family = "GECO", vintage = m[2], iam = NA, pathway = m[3],
                family_prefix = paste0("GECO", m[2])))
  }
  # IPR pattern: IPR{year}[Automotive]_{pathway}
  m <- regmatches(code, regexec("^IPR(\\d{4})(Automotive)?[_](.+)$", code))[[1]]
  if (length(m) == 4) {
    suffix <- if (nzchar(m[3])) m[3] else ""
    return(list(family = "IPR", vintage = m[2], iam = NA, pathway = m[4],
                family_prefix = paste0("IPR", m[2], suffix)))
  }
  # mission_possible pattern
  m <- regmatches(code, regexec("^mission_possible[_](.+)[_](baseline|NZ)$", code))[[1]]
  if (length(m) == 3) {
    return(list(family = "MissionPossible", vintage = NA, iam = NA, pathway = m[3],
                family_prefix = paste0("mission_possible_", m[2])))
  }
  NULL
}

Step 2: Add find_sensitivity_scenarios() function

Finds all sibling target scenarios in the same family+vintage:

#' Find sibling target scenarios for sensitivity analysis
#' @param target_code The user's selected target scenario code
#' @param all_targets Character vector of all available target scenario codes
#' @return list(scenarios, parsed_info, labels) or NULL if no siblings found
find_sensitivity_scenarios <- function(target_code, all_targets) {
  parsed <- parse_scenario_code(target_code)
  if (is.null(parsed)) return(NULL)

  # Find all targets in the same family + vintage
  siblings <- character()
  for (code in all_targets) {
    p <- parse_scenario_code(code)
    if (is.null(p)) next
    if (p$family == parsed$family && identical(p$vintage, parsed$vintage)) {
      siblings <- c(siblings, code)
    }
  }

  # Must have at least 2 scenarios (including the primary) for sensitivity to be meaningful
  if (length(siblings) < 2) return(NULL)

  # Build labels for display
  labels <- sapply(siblings, scenario_label, USE.NAMES = TRUE)
  categories <- sapply(siblings, scenario_category, USE.NAMES = TRUE)

  list(
    scenarios = siblings,
    primary = target_code,
    family = parsed$family,
    vintage = parsed$vintage,
    labels = labels,
    categories = categories,
    n_total = length(siblings),
    n_additional = length(siblings) - 1L
  )
}

Step 3: Commit

docs.title: Task 4: Build the Sensitivity Module (Server Logic)
docs.text:
Files: - Create: app/modules/mod_sensitivity.R - Modify: app/server.R (source the module, call setup function)

Step 1: Create mod_sensitivity.R

This module handles all sensitivity analysis logic: scenario detection, running, and results rendering. It follows the same pattern as mod_results_horizon.R.

The module should contain:

  1. setup_sensitivity <- function(input, output, session, rv) — main setup function

  2. Scenario detection observer: When rv$results changes AND rv$target_scenarios_all is available, call find_sensitivity_scenarios() to detect siblings. Store in rv$sensitivity_config.

  3. output$sensitivity_content_ui renderUI: Renders different states:

  4. No main results: "Run the main analysis first" empty state
  5. No sensitivity scenarios found: "No alternative scenarios found" message
  6. Pre-run: Show detected scenarios grouped by category, with "Run Sensitivity Analysis" button
  7. Running: Progress indicator

  8. Results ready: Summary cards + plots + table

  9. observeEvent(input$run_sensitivity, ...): The execution handler:

  10. Gets the core scenario pair's baseline from the main run
  11. Loops through all sensitivity scenarios x shock years
  12. Calls run_trisk_on_portfolio() for each (reusing same data prep as main run)
  13. Includes the primary scenario's results (from rv$results_by_year) in the combined dataframe
  14. Stores flat results in rv$sensitivity_results

  15. Computes per-scenario summary in rv$sensitivity_summary

  16. Results rendering outputs:

  17. output$sensitivity_summary_cards: KPI cards showing median, IQR, range
  18. output$plot_sensitivity_pd: Violin/dot plot of PD Change (%) across scenarios
  19. output$plot_sensitivity_el: Violin/dot plot of Expected Loss Change across scenarios
  20. output$plot_sensitivity_npv: Violin/dot plot of NPV Change (%) across scenarios
  21. output$sensitivity_comparison_table: DT table with one row per scenario, all KPIs

Key implementation details for the run handler:

observeEvent(input$run_sensitivity, {
  req(rv$sensitivity_config, rv$results_by_year, rv$portfolio, rv$assets)

  config <- rv$sensitivity_config
  scenarios_to_run <- config$scenarios
  shock_years <- sort(as.integer(names(rv$results_by_year)))

  # Determine baseline (same as core pair used in main run)
  core_baseline <- rv$results$baseline_scenario[1]
  core_sectors <- SECTOR_SCENARIO_DEFAULTS[["core"]]$sectors

  withProgress(message = "Running sensitivity analysis...", value = 0, {
    all_results <- list()
    total_runs <- length(scenarios_to_run) * length(shock_years)
    run_count <- 0

    for (scen in scenarios_to_run) {
      for (yr in shock_years) {
        run_count <- run_count + 1
        incProgress(1 / total_runs,
                    detail = paste0(scenario_label(scen), " -- year ", yr,
                                   " (", run_count, "/", total_runs, ")"))

        # Check if this is the primary scenario -- reuse existing results
        if (scen == config$primary) {
          yr_results <- rv$results_by_year[[as.character(yr)]]
          if (!is.null(yr_results)) {
            yr_results$sensitivity_scenario <- scen
            all_results[[paste0(scen, "_", yr)]] <- yr_results
          }
          next
        }

        tryCatch({
          # Determine correct baseline for this target
          bl <- baseline_for_scenario(scen, rv$available_baselines, core_baseline)

          # Geography fallback
          geo_result <- get_geography_for_pair(bl, input$scenario_geography)

          # Filter data to core sectors
          portfolio_subset <- rv$portfolio %>%
            dplyr::filter(.data$sector %in% core_sectors)
          assets_subset <- rv$assets %>%
            dplyr::filter(.data$sector %in% core_sectors)

          # Year range filter
          pair_scenarios <- rv$scenarios %>%
            dplyr::filter(.data$scenario %in% c(bl, scen),
                          .data$scenario_geography %in% geo_result$geography)
          if (nrow(pair_scenarios) > 0) {
            scen_min <- min(pair_scenarios$scenario_year)
            scen_max <- max(pair_scenarios$scenario_year)
            assets_subset <- assets_subset %>%
              dplyr::filter(.data$production_year >= scen_min,
                            .data$production_year <= scen_max)
          }

          raw <- run_trisk_on_portfolio(
            assets_data = assets_subset,
            scenarios_data = rv$scenarios,
            financial_data = rv$financial,
            carbon_data = rv$carbon,
            portfolio_data = portfolio_subset,
            baseline_scenario = bl,
            target_scenario = scen,
            scenario_geography = geo_result$geography,
            shock_year = yr,
            risk_free_rate = input$risk_free_rate,
            discount_rate = input$discount_rate,
            growth_rate = input$growth_rate,
            market_passthrough = input$market_passthrough
          )

          result_df <- extract_result_df(raw)
          result_df <- compute_el_columns(result_df)
          result_df$shock_year <- yr
          result_df$sensitivity_scenario <- scen
          result_df$baseline_scenario <- bl
          all_results[[paste0(scen, "_", yr)]] <- result_df
        }, error = function(e) {
          log_message(paste0("Sensitivity: ", scen, " year ", yr, " failed: ",
                            conditionMessage(e)))
        })
      }
    }

    rv$sensitivity_results <- dplyr::bind_rows(all_results)
    # Compute per-scenario summary (see Task 5)
  })
})

Step 2: Wire the module into server.R

In app/server.R, after the existing module source calls, add:

source("modules/mod_sensitivity.R", local = TRUE)

And after the existing setup_* calls, add:

setup_sensitivity(input, output, session, rv)

Note: extract_result_df and get_geography_for_pair are defined locally in the execute_trisk_analysis closure. These need to be either: (a) moved to global.R as shared helpers, or (b) duplicated in the module. Option (a) is cleaner — move extract_result_df to global.R.

Step 3: Move extract_result_df to global.R

Cut the extract_result_df function from server.R:1378-1387 and paste it into global.R (after compute_el_columns). This makes it available to both the main run handler and the sensitivity module.

Step 4: Commit

docs.title: Task 5: Build Sensitivity Results Visualizations
docs.text:
Files: - Modify: app/modules/mod_sensitivity.R (add plot and table outputs)

Step 1: Add per-scenario summary computation

After the run loop completes, compute per-scenario aggregated KPIs:

rv$sensitivity_summary <- rv$sensitivity_results %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    pd_change_pct = ifelse(pd_baseline != 0,
                           (pd_shock - pd_baseline) / pd_baseline * 100, 0)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(sensitivity_scenario, shock_year) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    scenario_label = scenario_label(sensitivity_scenario[1]),
    category = scenario_category(sensitivity_scenario[1]),
    avg_pd_change = if ("exposure_value_usd" %in% names(.)) {
      weighted.mean(pd_change_pct, exposure_value_usd, na.rm = TRUE)
    } else mean(pd_change_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_el_change = if ("expected_loss_difference" %in% names(.))
      sum(expected_loss_difference, na.rm = TRUE) else NA_real_,
    avg_npv_change = if ("crispy_perc_value_change" %in% names(.))
      mean(crispy_perc_value_change, na.rm = TRUE) * 100 else NA_real_,
    n_companies = dplyr::n_distinct(company_id),
    is_primary = sensitivity_scenario[1] == rv$sensitivity_config$primary,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

Step 2: Build summary cards

Show median and range of the per-scenario KPIs (across all scenarios x years):

output$sensitivity_summary_cards <- renderUI({
  req(rv$sensitivity_summary)
  ss <- rv$sensitivity_summary

  median_pd <- median(ss$avg_pd_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  range_pd <- range(ss$avg_pd_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  median_el <- median(ss$total_el_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  median_npv <- median(ss$avg_npv_change, na.rm = TRUE)

  fluidRow(
    make_card("Scenarios Tested", nrow(dplyr::distinct(ss, sensitivity_scenario))),
    make_card("Median PD Change", paste0(round(median_pd, 1), "%")),
    make_card("PD Change Range",
      paste0(round(range_pd[1], 0), "% to ", round(range_pd[2], 0), "%")),
    make_card("Median EL Change", format_currency(median_el)),
    make_card("Median NPV Change", paste0(round(median_npv, 1), "%"))
  )
})

Step 3: Build violin/dot plots

Three plots, each using geom_violin() for the distribution and geom_point() for individual scenario values. The primary scenario is highlighted with a distinct marker.

Group scenarios by NGFS category on the x-axis. Use geom_hline() to show the primary scenario's value as a reference line.

Same pattern for EL and NPV plots. Use trisk_plot_theme() + plotly_theme_colors() for consistent styling. Each plot should be wrapped in ggplotly() with PLOTLY_CONFIG and PLOTLY_HOVERLABEL.

Step 4: Build comparison table

DT table with one row per scenario x shock year, showing all KPIs. Primary scenario row highlighted in bold.

Step 5: Commit

docs.title: Data Dictionary
docs.text: Complete field definitions for all TRISK Desktop inputs and outputs.

docs.title: Portfolio
docs.text: Column Type Description company_id integer/string Unique company identifier company_name string Company name (used for display and fuzzy matching) sector string Sector classification (Power, Oil&Gas, Coal, Automotive, Steel) technology string Technology within sector (CoalCap, GasCap, RenewablesCap, etc.) country_iso2 string ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code exposure_value_usd numeric Loan exposure amount in USD term integer Loan maturity in years (1-5) loss_given_default numeric Loss Given Default ratio (0 to 1)

docs.title: Assets
