docs.title: Financial Features
docs.text: Column Type Description company_id integer Links to portfolio and assets pd numeric Current probability of default net_profit_margin numeric Net profit margin ratio debt_equity_ratio numeric Debt to equity ratio volatility numeric Asset value volatility (Merton model input)

docs.title: Scenarios
docs.text: Column Type Description scenario string Scenario code (e.g., NGFS2024GCAM_CP) scenario_type string baseline or target scenario_geography string Regional scope (Global, EU, US, etc.) sector string Sector covered technology string Technology covered scenario_year integer Year in the projection (2022-2100) scenario_price numeric Commodity/energy price projection price_unit string Price unit (USD per unit) scenario_pathway numeric Production/capacity pathway value pathway_unit string Pathway unit (GW, Mt, etc.) technology_type string carbontech or greentech scenario_provider string Source (NGFS2024, IPR2021, etc.) scenario_capacity_factor numeric Expected capacity factor in scenario

docs.title: PD Results (company-level)
docs.text: Column Type Description company_id integer Company identifier company_name string Company name sector string Sector technology string Technology pd_baseline numeric Probability of Default under baseline scenario pd_shock numeric Probability of Default under shock scenario pd_change numeric pd_shock - pd_baseline (positive = higher risk) pd_change_pct numeric Percentage change in PD exposure_value_usd numeric Loan exposure loss_given_default numeric LGD ratio exposure_at_default numeric exposure_value_usd * loss_given_default expected_loss_baseline numeric -(EAD * pd_baseline) expected_loss_shock numeric -(EAD * pd_shock) expected_loss_difference numeric -(EAD * (pd_shock - pd_baseline))

docs.title: NPV Results (company-level)
docs.text: Column Type Description net_present_value_baseline numeric NPV of future cash flows under baseline net_present_value_shock numeric NPV under shock scenario net_present_value_difference numeric shock - baseline (negative = value destruction) net_present_value_change numeric Percentage change in NPV

docs.title: Run Metadata
docs.text: Field Description run_id Unique identifier: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_<8-char-hash> baseline_scenario Selected baseline scenario code target_scenario Selected target scenario code scenario_geography Selected geography shock_years Array of shock years tested risk_free_rate DCF risk-free rate used discount_rate DCF discount rate used growth_rate DCF terminal growth rate used market_passthrough Carbon cost passthrough rate used carbon_price_model no_carbon_tax or carbon_tax model_version trisk.model package version data_checksums SHA-256 hashes of input data files

docs.title: Model Parameters
docs.text: TRISK Desktop uses a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model combined with a Merton structural credit risk model to translate climate scenario impacts into financial risk metrics.

docs.title: DCF Parameters
docs.text:
These parameters control the valuation model that converts production trajectory changes into NPV impacts:

Parameter Default Range Description Risk-Free Rate 0.02 0 - 0.10 The risk-free interest rate used for discounting future cash flows. Higher values reduce the present value of distant impacts, lowering sensitivity to long-horizon scenarios. Discount Rate 0.07 0 - 0.20 Rate at which future dividends are discounted. A higher discount rate reduces the present value impact of the transition shock. Growth Rate 0.03 0 - 0.10 Terminal growth rate of profits beyond the final scenario year. Used in the Gordon Growth Model for terminal value. Must be less than the discount rate.

docs.title: Carbon Pricing Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Default Range Description Market Passthrough 0.00 0 - 1.00 The fraction of carbon tax costs that the company can pass through to consumers. 0 = company absorbs all costs, 1 = consumers absorb all costs. Carbon Price Model No Carbon Tax — Whether to apply scenario-specific carbon prices to company emissions. When enabled, high-emission companies face additional cost pressure from carbon taxation.

docs.title: Step 1: Production trajectories
docs.text:
For each company-sector-technology combination, the model constructs two production trajectories:

  • Baseline trajectory - production levels following the business-as-usual scenario (e.g., Current Policies)
  • Shock trajectory - production levels following the target scenario (e.g., Net Zero 2050), applied from the shock year onward

docs.title: Step 2: Net Present Value
docs.text:
The DCF model computes the NPV of future cash flows under each trajectory:

NPV = Sum(t=1..T) [ Net_Profit(t) / (1 + discount_rate)^t ] + Terminal_Value

Where Terminal Value uses the Gordon Growth Model:

Terminal_Value = Net_Profit(T) * (1 + growth_rate) / (discount_rate - growth_rate)

The difference NPV_shock - NPV_baseline measures the economic impact of the transition on each company.

docs.title: Step 3: Probability of Default
docs.text: The Merton structural model translates NPV changes into PD changes. A company's distance-to-default decreases when its asset value (proxied by NPV) drops. The model uses the company's financial characteristics (debt-equity ratio, volatility, profit margin) from the financial features dataset.

docs.title: Step 4: Expected Loss
docs.text:
Expected Loss combines the PD change with exposure data:

EL = -(Exposure_at_Default * PD)

Where:

Exposure_at_Default = exposure_value_usd * loss_given_default

docs.title: Sensitivity to parameters
docs.text: If you increase... Effect on results Risk-Free Rate Lower NPV impact, lower PD sensitivity to distant shocks Discount Rate Lower NPV impact overall, particularly for long-horizon scenarios Growth Rate Higher terminal values, amplifies long-run differences between scenarios Market Passthrough Reduces carbon tax impact on company profits (costs passed to consumers) Shock Year (later) More accumulated misalignment, typically larger PD impacts

docs.title: Default values
docs.text:
The defaults are calibrated for a balanced analysis of European/global portfolios. For specific regional or institutional contexts, adjust based on your internal DCF assumptions.

To restore all defaults, click Reset All to Defaults on the Configure Analysis tab.

docs.title: Scenario Library
docs.text: TRISK Desktop includes 114 pre-loaded climate transition scenarios from four major providers.

docs.title: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System)
docs.text:
The primary scenario source for banking climate stress tests. NGFS scenarios are organized by policy ambition:

Category Scenarios Description Orderly (Net Zero) NGFS2024GCAM_NZ2050, NGFS2023GCAM_NZ2050 Early, coordinated policy action achieves net-zero by 2050 Orderly (Below 2C) NGFS2024GCAM_B2C Gradual policy tightening limits warming to below 2C Disorderly (Delayed) NGFS2024GCAM_DT Late policy action leads to disorderly transition after 2030 Hot House World NGFS2024GCAM_CP Current Policies - minimal additional action (used as baseline)

Sectors covered: Oil&Gas, Coal, Power

Geographies: Global, EU, US, Developing Economies, and regional groupings

Models: GCAM, REMIND-MAgPIE, MESSAGEix-GLOBIOM

docs.title: IPR (Inevitable Policy Response)
docs.text:
Scenarios focused on sectors with distinct transition pathways not well covered by NGFS:

Scenario Type Sector IPR2023Automotive_baseline Baseline Automotive IPR2023Automotive_FPS Target (Forecast Policy) Automotive IPR2021_FPS Target Power, Oil&Gas IPR2021_RPS Target (Required Policy) Power, Oil&Gas

Primary use: Automotive sector analysis where NGFS scenarios have limited coverage.

docs.title: Mission Possible Partnership
docs.text:
Deep decarbonization pathways for hard-to-abate industrial sectors:

Scenario Type Sector mission_possible_Steel_baseline Baseline Steel mission_possible_Steel_NZ Target (Net Zero) Steel

Primary use: Steel sector analysis.

docs.title: GECO (Global Energy and Climate Outlook)
docs.text: EU Joint Research Centre scenarios providing additional European policy perspectives.

docs.title: How scenarios are matched
docs.text:
The app automatically selects appropriate scenarios based on portfolio composition:

Portfolio sectors Default scenario pair Oil&Gas, Coal, Power NGFS GCAM Current Policies (baseline) vs. Net Zero 2050 (target) Automotive IPR Automotive baseline vs. Forecast Policy Scenario Steel Mission Possible baseline vs. Net Zero

If your portfolio spans multiple sector groups, the app creates separate scenario pairs for each group and runs them independently.

docs.title: Scenario structure
docs.text:
Each scenario provides year-by-year projections for:

  • Production/capacity pathways - how much each technology produces under the scenario
  • Energy/commodity prices - price projections under different policy assumptions
  • Carbon prices - the implicit or explicit carbon cost in each scenario

The model uses these projections to compute how company revenues, costs, and ultimately NPV change when transitioning from the baseline to the target scenario.

docs.title: Custom scenarios
docs.text:
You can upload your own scenario file with the same column structure as the bundled data. See the Data Dictionary for the required schema.

Custom scenarios appear in the baseline and target dropdown menus alongside the bundled scenarios.

docs.title: Scenario geographies
docs.text:
Available geographies depend on the scenario provider:

Geography NGFS IPR Mission Possible Global Yes Yes Yes EU / Europe Yes Limited No US / North America Yes Limited No Developing Economies Yes No No Country-specific Some No No

When a selected geography is not available for a specific scenario, the app falls back to Global.

docs.title: Step 1: Upload Data
docs.text: The Upload Data tab is where you load your portfolio and supporting data into TRISK Desktop.

docs.title: Quick start with demo data
docs.text:
The fastest way to try the app is the Load Demo Data button at the top of the page. This loads a synthetic portfolio (4 companies across Oil&Gas, Coal, and Power) with matching asset, financial, and scenario data from the trisk.model and trisk.analysis R packages.

Click Load Demo Data from trisk.model and you will see green checkmarks confirming each dataset loaded successfully:

  • Portfolio: 4 rows
  • Assets: 42 rows
  • Financial: 5 rows
  • Scenarios: 1,422 rows
  • Carbon prices: 1,376 rows

docs.title: Uploading your own data
docs.text:
Below the demo data section, four upload panels accept CSV files:

Portfolio Data (required)

Your loan book. Click Browse to select a CSV file. The app validates column names immediately after upload and reports any missing required columns.

Assets Data (recommended)

Company-level production and asset data. A comprehensive global dataset is bundled with the app. Upload your own only if you have proprietary asset-level data.

Financial Features (optional)

Company PD, profit margins, debt ratios, and volatility. Used by the Merton structural model to compute PD changes. If not provided, the app uses the bundled financial dataset.

Scenarios (optional)

114 scenarios from NGFS, IPR, GECO, and Mission Possible are pre-loaded. Upload only if you have custom scenario pathways.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After upload, the app runs validation checks:

  • Required columns present (case-sensitive match)
  • Numeric columns contain valid numbers
  • No entirely empty files
  • Row count within limits (max 500,000)
  • File size within limits (max 100 MB)

The Data Quality Overview panel at the bottom shows validation status for each dataset. If issues are found, download the Error Report CSV for details.

Column stripping

Extra columns beyond the expected set are automatically removed on upload. This is a security feature that prevents accidental retention of PII or sensitive data.

docs.title: Alternative: mount data via Docker
docs.text:
Instead of uploading through the browser, you can place CSV files in the data/input/ directory before starting the container. The app detects files at this path and makes them available for loading.

# Place your files
cp my_portfolio.csv data/input/
cp my_assets.csv data/input/

# Start the container (files are mounted read-only)
docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Once data is loaded, proceed to Step 2: Configure Analysis.

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Analysis
docs.text: The Configure Analysis tab is where you select climate scenarios, set model parameters, and define the shock timeline.

docs.title: Scenario selection
docs.text:
The Scenarios panel shows two dropdown menus:

Baseline (Business-as-Usual)

The reference scenario representing the current policy trajectory. Default: Current Policies from the NGFS GCAM model.

Target (Transition Goal)

The climate transition shock scenario. Default: Net Zero 2050 from the NGFS GCAM model.

The model computes the difference between these two scenarios to measure the transition risk impact. At the shock year, companies are forced from the baseline trajectory onto the target pathway.

Sector-aware scenario defaults

The app automatically detects which sectors are in your portfolio and selects appropriate scenarios:

  • Oil&Gas, Coal, Power - NGFS scenarios (default)
  • Automotive - IPR scenarios
  • Steel - Mission Possible scenarios

If your portfolio spans multiple sectors, the app creates separate scenario pairs for each sector group.

docs.title: Scenario geography
docs.text: Select the regional scope for scenario pathways. Options include Global, EU, US, Developing Economies, and others depending on the loaded scenario data. Default: Global.

docs.title: Shock scenario year
docs.text:
Select one or more shock years between 2025 and 2040. The shock year determines when companies are forced from the baseline trajectory onto the target pathway.

  • A single shock year produces a single-point stress test
  • Multiple shock years (e.g., 2025, 2030, 2035) enable the Horizon Analysis tab in the results, showing how risk evolves over time

docs.title: Model parameters
docs.text:
The Model Parameters panel controls the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation:

Parameter Default Range What it controls Risk-Free Rate 0.02 0 - 0.10 Interest rate used in DCF discounting Discount Rate 0.07 0 - 0.20 Discount rate of dividends per year Growth Rate 0.03 0 - 0.10 Terminal growth rate beyond the final year Market Passthrough 0.00 0 - 1.00 Ability to pass carbon costs to consumers (0 = none, 1 = full)

docs.title: Carbon price model
docs.text:
Choose whether to include carbon pricing effects:

  • No Carbon Tax (default) - carbon prices are not applied to company emissions
  • Apply Carbon Tax - scenario-specific carbon prices are applied, reducing profits for high-emission companies based on the market passthrough rate

docs.title: Reset to defaults
docs.text: Click Reset All to Defaults to restore all parameters to their original values.

docs.title: Configuration summary
docs.text: The Run Analysis tab (next step) displays a summary of your selected configuration, so you can verify before executing.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Once configured, proceed to Step 3: Run Analysis.

docs.title: Step 3: Run Analysis
docs.text: The Run Analysis tab executes the TRISK stress test on your portfolio.

docs.title: Pre-run checklist
docs.text:
Before the Run button activates, the app verifies:

  • Portfolio data uploaded
  • Assets data uploaded
  • Financial data uploaded
  • Scenarios data available
  • Carbon price data available
  • Baseline and target scenarios selected
  • Portfolio sectors detected
  • At least one valid scenario pair

All items must show green checkmarks. If any show red, go back to the Upload or Configure tabs to resolve.

docs.title: Configuration summary
docs.text:
The right panel displays the exact parameters that will be used:

Core Energy (NGFS): Net Zero 2050
Geography: Global
Shock Year(s): 2025, 2030, 2035
Risk-Free Rate: 0.02
Discount Rate: 0.07
Growth Rate: 0.03
Market Passthrough: 0
Carbon Price Model: no_carbon_tax

Verify this matches your intent before proceeding.

docs.title: Running the model
docs.text:
Click the Run TRISK Analysis button. The model executes these steps:

  1. Match portfolio companies to the assets database by company_id
  2. Compute production trajectories under baseline and shock scenarios for each company-sector-technology
  3. Derive PD changes using the Merton structural model - comparing company NPV under both scenarios
  4. Calculate Expected Loss = -(Exposure at Default x PD) for both scenarios

The Analysis Log at the bottom shows real-time progress. A typical 100-company portfolio takes 30-60 seconds. Larger portfolios (500+ companies) may take several minutes.

docs.title: Run ID
docs.text:
Each run generates a unique identifier in the format:

YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_<8-char-hash>

For example: 20260325_231552_eb1600eb

This ID is attached to all results and exports, enabling full traceability and reproducibility. The hash component is derived from the run configuration, so identical parameters produce the same hash prefix.

docs.title: What if the run fails?
docs.text:
Common causes:

  • No matching companies - portfolio company_ids don't appear in the assets database. Check that IDs match across files.
  • Scenario not available for geography - the selected scenario may not cover your chosen geography. Try "Global" or check the Scenario Library.
  • Out of memory - for large portfolios, increase Docker memory allocation (Settings > Resources > Memory).

The Analysis Log will show warning messages for any sector/year combinations that failed, while successful combinations still produce results.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: After the run completes, the app automatically navigates to Step 4: Portfolio Results.

docs.title: Step 4: Portfolio Results
docs.text: The Portfolio Results tab is the main output screen. It shows headline metrics, detailed breakdowns, and interactive charts across five sub-tabs.

docs.title: Headline value boxes
docs.text:
Five KPI cards appear at the top of the page:

Metric What it shows Companies Number of portfolio companies with results Total Exposure Sum of all exposure_value_usd in your portfolio Max PD Shock Highest single-company PD shock value PD Change Portfolio-weighted average PD change (basis points) Total EL Total Expected Loss under the shock scenario

docs.title: Summary tab
docs.text:
A table showing sector/technology-level aggregates:

  • Number of companies per sector-technology combination
  • Average shocked PD
  • Average PD change
  • Total exposure
  • Total Expected Loss

Use this tab for the portfolio overview that goes into ICAAP summary tables and board presentations.

docs.title: Credit Risk Analysis tab
docs.text:
Detailed company-level credit risk view:

PD Change by Sector bar chart

Interactive plotly chart showing PD shock magnitude for each company, colored by sector. Red bars indicate PD increase (higher default risk), green bars indicate PD decrease (lower risk).

Company-level PD table

Full results table with sortable columns: company name, sector, technology, baseline PD, shocked PD, PD change, percentage change, exposure, Expected Loss.

PD Distribution histogram

Distribution of PD changes across all portfolio companies. Shows the spread of transition risk impact - how many companies see large PD increases versus moderate or negligible changes.

docs.title: Risk Concentration tab
docs.text:
Measures how concentrated transition risk is across your portfolio:

Concentration metrics

HHI Index (Herfindahl-Hirschman), Top-5 and Top-10 concentration ratios, maximum single-name weight, number of sectors represented.

Concentration Heatmap

Visual matrix showing exposure concentration by sector and PD impact level. Darker cells indicate higher concentration of risk in specific sector-impact combinations.

Risk-Return Scatter

Each bubble represents one company. The x-axis shows PD change, y-axis shows exposure weight in the portfolio, and bubble size represents the absolute EL change. Companies in the upper-right quadrant have both high exposure weight and large PD increases - the highest risk contributors.

docs.title: Attribution tab
docs.text:
Decomposes the portfolio-level PD change into sector and company contributions:

PD Waterfall chart

Starts at baseline portfolio PD, shows each sector's contribution to the total change, and lands at the final shocked PD. This answers: "Which sectors are driving the portfolio risk increase?"

Sector attribution table

Quantitative breakdown of each sector's exposure-weighted contribution to portfolio PD change.

docs.title: Horizon Analysis tab
docs.text:
Requires 2+ shock years

This tab only activates when you selected multiple shock years in the Configure step (e.g., 2025, 2030, 2035).

Shows how risk evolves across different shock year horizons:

Year-over-Year Metrics table

For each shock year: average PD, maximum PD, total EL, EL change from previous year, number of affected companies, and PD delta between consecutive years. Later shock years typically show larger impacts as companies have longer to accumulate misalignment.

Horizon charts

Line charts showing PD and EL evolution across shock years at sector and portfolio level.

docs.title: Exporting from results
docs.text: Each sub-tab includes an Export CSV button that downloads the specific table shown. For a full export of all results, use the Download tab.

docs.title: Run history
docs.text: If you run the analysis multiple times (e.g., with different scenarios or parameters), the run history panel lets you compare up to 5 previous runs side by side.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: To compare results across multiple scenario families, proceed to Step 5: Scenario Analysis. To integrate with your internal PD/EL, skip to Step 6: Risk Integration.

docs.title: Step 5: Scenario Analysis
docs.text: The Scenario Analysis tab runs a sensitivity analysis across multiple climate scenario families to compare how different transition pathways affect your portfolio.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: Instead of testing a single baseline-target pair (as in Step 3), this tab runs your portfolio against multiple target scenarios while keeping the same baseline. This answers: "How sensitive is my portfolio to the severity of the transition?"

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Select Policy Groups to Analyze

The app automatically detects which scenario families are available based on your loaded scenario data and groups them by ambition level (Net Zero, Below 2C, Delayed Transition, Current Policies). Check the boxes for the groups you want to compare.

Your scenario is marked with a star icon - this is the scenario pair you already ran in Step 3.

The analysis uses the same shock years, geography, and model parameters from your Step 2 configuration.

docs.title: Running the sensitivity analysis
docs.text: Click Run Sensitivity Analysis. The app runs the TRISK model once per selected scenario family, reusing the same portfolio, assets, and financial data. This takes longer than a single run - roughly proportional to the number of selected scenarios.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
After completion, the tab displays:

Scenario Comparison table

Side-by-side metrics (average PD, max PD, total EL, exposure at risk) for each scenario family. Highlights which transition pathway produces the largest portfolio impact.

Distribution comparison charts

PD distributions overlaid across scenario families, showing how the spread of company-level impacts varies by transition ambition.

Sector-level sensitivity

How each sector responds differently to mild versus aggressive transition scenarios. Some sectors (e.g., Coal) may show large PD changes across all scenarios, while others (e.g., Power with renewables) may benefit under aggressive transition pathways.

Interpreting scenario families

  • Net Zero 2050: Aggressive, orderly transition - largest PD impact on carbon-intensive companies
  • Below 2C: Moderate transition ambition
  • Delayed Transition: Late, disorderly action - shock concentrated in later years
  • Current Policies: Minimal additional policy action - lowest transition risk, but highest physical risk (not modeled here)

docs.title: When to use this tab
docs.text:
• Regulatory submissions requiring multi-scenario analysis (ECB, PRA guidelines)
  • Board presentations showing the range of possible outcomes
  • Risk appetite discussions comparing mild versus severe transition impacts
  • ICAAP stress test documentation with scenario sensitivity evidence

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: To combine TRISK outputs with your internal PD/EL estimates, proceed to Step 6: Risk Integration. To export results directly, skip to Step 7: Download.

docs.title: Step 6: Risk Integration
docs.text:
The Risk Integration tab is an optional step where you combine TRISK climate stress test outputs with your bank's internally calculated PD and Expected Loss estimates.

Skip this step if you only need the raw TRISK results from Step 4.

docs.title: Why integrate?
docs.text:
TRISK produces PD changes relative to its own baseline scenario. Your bank likely has its own PD estimates from internal rating models. Integration combines both:

  • TRISK tells you how much PD changes due to climate transition
  • Your internal model tells you what the current PD is for each borrower
  • Integration produces an adjusted PD that incorporates both

docs.title: Choose a method
docs.text:
Three methods are available, each suited to a different institutional context:

Absolute Change

Best when your internal PDs are point-in-time estimates. Adds the raw TRISK PD delta to your internal PD.

Adjusted PD = Internal PD + (PD_shock - PD_baseline)
Relative Change

Best when your internal PDs are through-the-cycle estimates. Applies a proportional scaling that respects your existing PD level.

Adjusted PD = Internal PD * (1 + PD_change%)
Z-Score (Basel IRB)

For Basel III/IV IRB-aligned institutions using the Vasicek one-factor model. Combines PDs in normal quantile space, preserving tail-risk behavior.

Adjusted PD = Phi(Phi_inv(Internal) + Phi_inv(Shock) - Phi_inv(Baseline))

Recommended for regulatory capital calculations.

docs.title: Providing your internal PD values
docs.text:
Two options:

  1. Upload CSV - prepare a file with company_id and internal_pd columns (values between 0 and 1)
  2. Edit in table - the app shows a pre-filled table where you can type or paste values directly

docs.title: Integration results
docs.text:
After selecting a method and providing internal PDs, the app displays:

  • Company-level adjusted PD table - baseline, TRISK shock, internal, and adjusted PD side by side
  • Portfolio aggregates - exposure-weighted average adjusted PD, sector breakdowns
  • Adjustment bar chart - visual comparison of internal versus adjusted PD per company

docs.title: EL Integration
docs.text:
The Expected Loss section works similarly, with two methods:

Absolute Change

Adjusted EL = Internal EL + (EL_shock - EL_baseline)

Relative Change

Adjusted EL = Internal EL * (EL_shock / EL_baseline)

Upload a CSV with company_id and internal_el columns, or edit values directly.

docs.title: Export
docs.text: Integration results are included in the Excel and CSV exports on the Download tab, alongside the raw TRISK results. The exported file clearly labels which columns are TRISK outputs and which are integrated values.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Proceed to Step 7: Download & Export to export all results.

docs.title: Step 7: Download & Export
docs.text: The Download tab provides all export options and run metadata for audit documentation.

docs.title: Export formats
docs.text:
Download All Results (Excel)

Multi-sheet XLSX workbook containing:

  • Summary - portfolio-level KPIs by sector and technology
  • Full Results - company-level baseline PD, shocked PD, PD change, Expected Loss, NPV
  • PD Integration - adjusted PD values (if Step 6 was used)
  • EL Integration - adjusted EL values (if Step 6 was used)
Download Results (CSV)

Flat CSV with all company-level results in a single file. Best for pipeline integration or further analysis in R, Python, or Excel.

Download Results (JSON)

Machine-readable structured output. Includes results array and metadata. Best for API integration or archival.

Download HTML Report

Self-contained HTML summary with embedded charts. Can be opened in any browser and shared by email without requiring TRISK Desktop.

docs.title: Analysis metadata
docs.text:
The right panel shows run metadata:

  • Run ID - unique identifier for this analysis run (format: YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS_<hash>)
  • Timestamp - exact date and time the analysis was executed
  • Scenario Pairs - which baseline/target combinations were used
  • Geography - scenario geography scope
  • Results rows - number of company-level result rows

docs.title: Download Configuration
docs.text:
Click Download Configuration to save a JSON file with the complete run configuration:

{
  "run_id": "20260325_231552_eb1600eb",
  "baseline_scenario": "NGFS2023GCAM_CP",
  "target_scenario": "NGFS2023GCAM_NZ2050",
  "scenario_geography": "Global",
  "shock_years": [2025, 2030, 2035],
  "risk_free_rate": 0.02,
  "discount_rate": 0.07,
  "growth_rate": 0.03,
  "market_passthrough": 0,
  "carbon_price_model": "no_carbon_tax",
  "model_version": "2.6.1",
  "data_checksums": { ... }
}

This file enables full reproducibility - anyone with the same input data and configuration can replicate the exact results.

docs.title: Security notes
docs.text:
• All exports pass through a column allowlist - only expected result columns are included, never raw uploaded data or internal fields
  • Character columns are sanitized against formula injection (CWE-1236) - cells starting with =, +, @, or tab characters are prefixed with a single quote to prevent Excel formula execution
  • Exports are written to the data/output/ directory inside the container

docs.title: Using exports for regulatory reporting
docs.text:
For ICAAP and regulatory submissions:

  1. Download the Excel file for the main results workbook
  2. Download the Configuration JSON as audit evidence of parameters used
  3. The Run ID ties all outputs to a specific analysis execution
  4. If integration was used, the Excel file includes both raw TRISK and adjusted columns clearly labeled
